content: Before the `@` syntax existed, Python programmers already wrapped functions by hand — assigning `fn = deco(fn)` after the definition. This worked but placed the decoration far below the function name, hurting readability. The `@decorator` syntax was added to make that intent explicit and visible right above the definition.

timeline.label: Manual wrapping
timeline.description: Developers reassigned names like `greet = shout(greet)` after defining the function.

timeline.label: PEP 318 (Python 2.4, 2004)
timeline.description: Introduced the @decorator syntax for functions and methods as sugar for that manual reassignment.

timeline.label: PEP 3129 (Python 3.0)
timeline.description: Extended decorator syntax to classes, not just functions.

timeline.label: PEP 614 (Python 3.9, 2020)
timeline.description: Relaxed the grammar so any valid expression can appear after @, not just a dotted name.
